title: Philippians 1
subtitle: Thanksgiving, Prayer, and Living for Christ
prayer_prompt: As Paul thanks God for the Philippians' partnership in the gospel, consider asking God who He's given you as partners in the work — and whether you've told them lately.

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — "Partners in the Gospel from the First Day"

heb.paragraph: The noun koinōnia (v. 5) denotes not merely social association but active partnership and shared participation. Paul thanks God for the Philippians’ ‘partnership in the gospel from the first day until now.’ This partnership includes financial support (4:15–16), prayer (1:19), and shared suffering (1:29–30). The term establishes the relational foundation of the entire letter.

In Paul’s prayer (v. 9), he asks that the Philippians’ love may abound ‘in knowledge and depth of insight’ (en epignōsei kai pasē aisthēsei). Epignōsis is not abstract intellectual knowledge but discerning, experiential understanding that shapes moral judgment. Love without discernment is sentimentality; Paul prays for love guided by wisdom.

refs.note: Paul’s thanksgiving for the Thessalonians’ ‘work produced by faith, labour prompted by love, and endurance inspired by hope’ parallels the partnership language of Phil 1:5.
The Philippians’ financial partnership, referenced in the thanksgiving (1:5), is detailed in the letter’s closing: they were the only church to share with Paul in the matter of giving and receiving.

notes.note: Paul identifies himself as a ‘servant’ (doulos) of Christ Jesus, not an apostle—reflecting the letter’s intimate, non-polemical tone. The mention of ‘overseers and deacons’ is unique among Pauline salutations and may acknowledge those who organised the financial gift.

Verse 6 is one of the great assurance texts: God’s completion of his saving work is as certain as its commencement. MacArthur emphasises that perseverance is ultimately God’s work, not the believer’s achievement.

Paul’s prayer is for discerning love that produces moral excellence, resulting in a harvest of righteousness for the glory of God. The progression is significant: love produces insight, insight produces moral choices, moral choices produce righteousness, and righteousness glorifies God.

Calvin reads this verse as a foundation for the doctrine of perseverance: God does not begin a work he will not finish. Election guarantees completion. Calvin notes that the ‘good work’ is God’s entire saving operation—from conversion through sanctification to glorification.

Calvin observes that Paul does not pray for love alone but for love with knowledge. Uninstructed zeal is dangerous; informed love is the mark of spiritual maturity. The fruit of righteousness is ‘through Jesus Christ’—not produced by human effort but mediated through union with Christ.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The terms episkopois (overseers) and diakonois (deacons/servants) are non-technical at this stage, likely referring to functions rather than formal offices. The NET translators note the absence of the later hierarchical distinctions.

The articular participle ho enarxamenos (the one who began) identifies God as the subject: it is God who initiated the saving work. The verb epitelesō (to complete, perfect) assures that the work will reach its appointed end at the parousia.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, Philippians, NICNT (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee reads the thanksgiving as establishing the letter’s relational foundation: the Philippians are Paul’s partners, not merely his converts. The term koinōnia in v. 5 is the key: it encompasses every dimension of their shared life in the gospel. Fee notes the eschatological framework: the ‘good work’ of v. 6 is oriented toward ‘the day of Christ Jesus,’ and the prayer of vv. 9–11 envisions growth that culminates in eschatological vindication.

silva.source: Moisés Silva, Philippians, BECNT (2nd ed., 2005) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Silva identifies the thanksgiving as both grateful and strategic: by recalling the Philippians’ consistent partnership, Paul lays the groundwork for the appeals to unity and sacrifice that will follow. Silva notes that the prayer’s request for discerning love (v. 9) connects directly to the community’s internal tensions (4:2–3): the Philippians need wisdom to navigate disagreements without fracturing their fellowship.

hist.context: Paul’s opening is the warmest in his corpus. Unlike Galatians (no thanksgiving) or Romans (formal self-introduction), Philippians radiates personal affection. Paul addresses the congregation together with ‘overseers and deacons’ (episkopois kai diakonois, v. 1)—the only Pauline salutation that mentions church officers. The thanksgiving (vv. 3–8) expresses deep gratitude for the Philippians’ consistent partnership, grounded in Paul’s confidence that ‘he who began a good work in you will carry it on to completion until the day of Christ Jesus’ (v. 6)—a statement of divine perseverance. The prayer (vv. 9–11) asks for discerning love, moral purity, and the fruit of righteousness, all oriented toward ‘the glory and praise of God’ (v. 11).

heb.paragraph: The noun prokopē (v. 12) is a military term for advance through difficult terrain. Paul’s imprisonment, far from hindering the gospel, has ‘actually served to advance’ it. The paradox—chains leading to progress—encapsulates the letter’s theology of divine reversal.

refs.note: Paul’s assertion that ‘God’s word is not chained’ parallels Phil 1:12–14: imprisonment restrains the apostle but not the gospel.
Paul’s confidence about being ‘away from the body and at home with the Lord’ parallels the desire in Phil 1:21–23 to depart and be with Christ.

notes.note: Paul transforms the narrative of his imprisonment: what appears to be a setback is actually a strategic advance. The whole imperial guard has heard the gospel. MacArthur notes that God uses opposition as a catalyst for mission.

Paul’s generosity toward rival preachers is remarkable: even those who preach from selfish ambition advance the gospel. MacArthur cautions that Paul endorses the content (Christ preached), not the motives.

Paul’s confidence rests on two resources: the Philippians’ prayers and ‘the help given by the Spirit of Jesus Christ.’ His goal is not survival but that Christ be magnified in his body, ‘whether by life or by death.’

Calvin draws the principle that God’s providence overrules human schemes: the enemies of the gospel cannot prevent its advance; even their opposition serves God’s purposes. Calvin notes the rare magnanimity of Paul’s attitude: he rejoices that Christ is preached, regardless of the preacher’s motives.

Calvin reads ‘Christ will be exalted in my body’ as the believer’s ultimate vocation: whether living or dying, the goal is the same—the glory of Christ. The body is not the believer’s own possession but Christ’s instrument.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase en holō tō praitōriō (in the whole praetorium/palace guard) is debated: it may refer to the Praetorian Guard (imperial bodyguard), the praetorium (governor’s residence), or an official building. The NET favours the Praetorian Guard, consistent with the Roman provenance.

The expression ti gar (what then?, what does it matter?) introduces Paul’s climactic response. The double plhn hoti (only that, except that) indicates that Paul’s sole concern is the proclamation of Christ, regardless of the preacher’s character.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, Philippians, NICNT (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee emphasises the paradox: Paul’s chains have become a pulpit. The gospel advances not despite suffering but through it. Fee notes that the rival preachers (vv. 15–17) are not heretics but Christians with personal animosity toward Paul, yet Paul refuses to let personal grievance eclipse the gospel’s progress.

silva.source: Moisés Silva, Philippians, BECNT (2nd ed., 2005) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Silva reads vv. 12–26 as a unified section addressing the question the Philippians would naturally ask: how is Paul doing? His answer reframes their concern christologically: his circumstances matter only insofar as they serve the gospel. Silva notes the rhetorical function of v. 21 (‘to live is Christ, to die is gain’): it establishes the ethical paradigm that grounds the Christ-hymn of ch. 2.

hist.context: Paul reports on his circumstances: his imprisonment has advanced the gospel in two ways. First, it has become known throughout the imperial guard (praitoriō, v. 13) that Paul is in chains for Christ. Second, most believers have been emboldened to speak the word of God more courageously (v. 14). Paul acknowledges that some preach Christ from envy and rivalry (v. 15), seeking to add affliction to his imprisonment (v. 17). His response is remarkable: ‘what does it matter? The important thing is that in every way, whether from false motives or true, Christ is preached’ (v. 18). Paul’s confidence extends to his personal situation: whether by life or death, Christ will be exalted in his body (v. 20). The famous declaration of v. 21—‘to live is Christ and to die is gain’—captures his indifference to personal outcome in service of the gospel.

sections.header: Verses 21–30 — "Live in a Manner Worthy of the Gospel"

heb.paragraph: The verb politeuesthe (v. 27, cognate of politeuma in 3:20) means ‘live as citizens’ or ‘conduct your public life.’ Philippi was a Roman colony whose residents were proud of their Roman citizenship. Paul appropriates the political vocabulary: the Philippians’ true citizenship is in heaven (3:20), and their public conduct must be ‘worthy of the gospel of Christ.’

refs.note: Paul’s statement about filling up ‘what is still lacking in regard to Christ’s afflictions’ parallels Phil 1:29–30, where suffering for Christ is presented as a privilege and a form of fellowship with Christ.
Peter’s teaching that believers should ‘rejoice inasmuch as you participate in the sufferings of Christ’ provides a parallel to Paul’s theology of suffering as a gift in Phil 1:29.

notes.note: Paul’s dilemma is genuine: departure would bring him into Christ’s immediate presence, but remaining serves the church. MacArthur notes that Paul’s desire to depart proves consciousness after death—there is no soul sleep in Pauline eschatology.

The call to ‘conduct yourselves in a manner worthy of the gospel’ introduces the letter’s ethical section. Standing firm ‘in one spirit’ addresses the disunity hinted at throughout the letter. Suffering is reframed as a gift: it has been ‘granted’ (charizo—same root as grace) to the Philippians, placing suffering and faith on the same plane as divine gifts.

Calvin reads Paul’s desire to depart as evidence that the soul’s blessedness begins immediately at death, not at the final resurrection. Being ‘with Christ’ is the intermediate state between death and resurrection—incomplete but far better than earthly existence.

Calvin finds in this verse a counter-intuitive doctrine: suffering for Christ is a gift of grace. Just as faith is a divine gift, so is the privilege of suffering. Calvin warns against the Stoic error of treating suffering as merely indifferent: Christian suffering is meaningful because it participates in Christ’s own sufferings.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb politeuesthe (live as citizens) is unusual in the NT (cf. Acts 23:1). The NET translators note that it was especially resonant in Philippi, a Roman colony whose citizens prided themselves on their Roman civic identity.

The verb echaristhē (it has been granted) derives from charis (grace). The aorist passive indicates a divine gift: God is the implied agent who bestows both faith and suffering. The double to hyper Christou (on behalf of Christ) frames both believing and suffering as Christ-oriented.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, Philippians, NICNT (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee reads vv. 27–30 as the letter’s first direct exhortation, grounded in the paradigm of vv. 12–26: just as Paul’s circumstances serve the gospel, so the Philippians’ public conduct must embody the gospel. Fee notes that the suffering theology of v. 29 is participatory: suffering for Christ is not merely endured but granted as a form of fellowship with Christ’s own path.

silva.source: Moisés Silva, Philippians, BECNT (2nd ed., 2005) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Silva identifies v. 27 as the letter’s thesis statement: ‘conduct yourselves in a manner worthy of the gospel.’ Everything that follows—the Christ-hymn, the practical instructions, the warnings—unpacks this call. Silva notes that the political vocabulary (politeuesthe) is deliberately provocative in a Roman colony: the Philippians’ primary allegiance is not to Caesar but to Christ.

hist.context: Paul’s personal dilemma (vv. 21–26)—torn between departing to be with Christ (far better) and remaining in the flesh (more needful for the Philippians)—resolves in favour of continued ministry: he is ‘convinced that I will remain’ (v. 25). The practical exhortation begins in v. 27: the Philippians must conduct themselves in a manner worthy of the gospel, standing firm in one spirit, contending as one person for the faith. The section closes with a theology of suffering: the Philippians have been ‘granted’ (echaristhē) not only to believe in Christ but also to suffer for him (v. 29). Suffering is a gift (charis), not a punishment—a perspective that governs the entire letter.

rows.label: vv.1–2
rows.text: Salutation: servants of Christ to the saints at Philippi

rows.label: vv.3–8
rows.text: Thanksgiving: partnership in the gospel from the first day

rows.label: vv.9–11
rows.text: Prayer: love abounding in knowledge and insight

rows.label: vv.12–18
rows.text: Paul’s chains advance the gospel; Christ preached regardless

rows.label: vv.19–26
rows.text: To live is Christ, to die is gain; remaining for their progress

rows.label: vv.27–30
rows.text: Conduct worthy of the gospel; suffering as a gift

lit.note: Philippians 1 moves from thanksgiving and prayer (vv. 1–11) through Paul’s report on his circumstances (vv. 12–26) to the first ethical exhortation (vv. 27–30). The chapter establishes the letter’s controlling themes: partnership, joy in suffering, and conduct worthy of the gospel.

themes.note: Mission dominates as Paul celebrates the gospel’s advance through his imprisonment. Faith is high through the partnership theme and the gift of believing. Sovereignty undergirds Paul’s confidence that God will complete his saving work.

hebtext.note: The noun koinōnia (v. 5) denotes not merely social association but active partnership and shared participation. Paul thanks God for the Philippians’ ‘partnership in the gospel from the first day until now.’ This partnership includes financial support (4:15–16), prayer (1:19), and shared suffering (1:29–30). The term establishes the relational foundation of the entire letter.

In Paul’s prayer (v. 9), he asks that the Philippians’ love may abound ‘in knowledge and depth of insight’ (en epignōsei kai pasē aisthēsei). Epignōsis is not abstract intellectual knowledge but discerning, experiential understanding that shapes moral judgment. Love without discernment is sentimentality; Paul prays for love guided by wisdom.

The noun prokopē (v. 12) is a military term for advance through difficult terrain. Paul’s imprisonment, far from hindering the gospel, has ‘actually served to advance’ it. The paradox—chains leading to progress—encapsulates the letter’s theology of divine reversal.

The verb politeuesthe (v. 27, cognate of politeuma in 3:20) means ‘live as citizens’ or ‘conduct your public life.’ Philippi was a Roman colony whose residents were proud of their Roman citizenship. Paul appropriates the political vocabulary: the Philippians’ true citizenship is in heaven (3:20), and their public conduct must be ‘worthy of the gospel of Christ.’

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

trans.title: Comparing translations in Thanksgiving, Prayer, and Living for Christ

translations.text: In all my prayers for all of you, I always pray with joy
always in every prayer of mine for you all making my prayer with joy,
It is right for me to feel this way about all of you, since I have you in my heart and, whether I am in chains or defending and confirming the gospel, all of you share in God’s grace with me.

It is right for me to feel this way about you all, because I hold you in my heart, for you are all partakers with me of grace, both in my imprisonment and in the defense and confirmation of the gospel.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Thanksgiving, Prayer, and Living for Christ within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

discourse.thesis: Paul's imprisonment has advanced the gospel — to live is Christ, to die is gain.

nodes.text: Paul thanks God for their partnership in the gospel from the first day until now. He holds them in his heart; they share in grace with him.
Paul prays their love may abound more and more — approving what is excellent, filled with the fruit of righteousness.
Paul's imprisonment has advanced the gospel. Most brothers have become bold to speak. Some preach from envy, some from love — either way, Christ is proclaimed.
Whether by life or death, Christ will be honored in Paul's body. To live is Christ; to die is gain. He is hard pressed between the two — but to remain is more necessary for them.
Let your manner of life be worthy of the gospel. Stand firm in one spirit, striving side by side for the faith. Suffering for Christ is granted to you.

coaching.tip: Paul writes from prison yet thanks God 'in all my remembrance of you' (v. 3) and expresses confidence in 'him who began a good work in you' (v. 6). Joy in chains — this disconnect between circumstance and emotion is the letter's theme. Philippians teaches that joy isn't happiness; it's deep confidence in God's completing work.

Paul's imprisonment has 'served to advance the gospel' (v. 12) — even the praetorian guard knows he's there for Christ, and other believers speak more boldly. What enemies meant for silencing became a megaphone. This reframing appears throughout Scripture: Joseph's pit, Daniel's den, the cross itself. God's kingdom advances through apparent setbacks.

'To live is Christ, and to die is gain' (v. 21) — Paul is genuinely torn between departure (better for him) and remaining (better for them). This isn't morbidity but settled identity: if his whole life is Christ, death simply intensifies the connection. The statement clarifies what 'to live is Christ' actually means. Death tests our slogans.
